findings.message: D1.1 says the ADR must not contain full trait/struct/method specifications, but D2.1 immediately embeds concrete Rust struct definitions for `SpecElementId`, `AdrAnchor`, `ConventionAnchor`, `ContentHash`, and the three ref types. That makes the ADR internally inconsistent about how much type detail it is allowed to spell out.

`SpecElementId` is only validated as a non-empty string in the task/verification text, but the ADR already fixes an ID naming convention (`IN-\d+`, `AC-\d+`, etc.). As written, the implementation can accept arbitrary ids, which weakens `SpecRef.anchor` and the new spec-element identity scheme.

The scope for T001/verification includes `track/workflow.md`, but the ADR rollout section only names `CLAUDE.md` and `DEVELOPER_AI_WORKFLOW.md`. That leaves one of the declared in-scope docs outside the ADR's own migration plan, so the file set is inconsistent across the documents.

D1.5 permits type catalogues to reference conventions, but the concrete ref schema only places `ConventionRef` on spec.json fields and D3.2 later says catalogues have no convention reference field at all. That gives implementers conflicting requirements: either catalogue entries need a `convention_refs` field and validator coverage, or the D1.5 permission must be removed.

The ADR lists `track/items/review-scope-prompt-injection-2026-04-18/review.json` as an observed-data reference, while the same ADR later forbids ADRs and new tracks from directly referencing track-internal artifacts to preserve lifecycle boundaries. The document should either move/summarize that evidence into a track-crossing artifact or define an explicit exception for historical observation data.

D2.3 says canonical-block detection implementation details are in Q15, but Q15 is about AdrAnchor/ConventionAnchor semantics and hash fields; Q14 is the canonical-block section. This broken reference points the T011/CLI implementation at the wrong design question.

D0.1 says any 🔴/🟡 SoT signal must trigger repair of the next upstream layer, but D3.1 and the /track:plan flow later define 🟡 as allowable and continue to the next phase. Implementers cannot satisfy both semantics: the orchestrator either loops on yellow or records a warning and proceeds.

The merge gate only requires task-coverage.json to cover all acceptance_criteria, while D1.4 defines coverage enforcement as covering both in_scope and acceptance_criteria. This weakens the final PR requirement relative to the task-coverage gate and leaves two conflicting definitions of what coverage is required for merge.

D4 says writers/capabilities must not span multiple phases, but the ownership table assigns the planner to both Phase 1 (/track:spec) and Phase 3 (/track:impl-plan). Since D1.4 also says the planner writes spec in Phase 1 and impl-plan/task-coverage in Phase 3, the writer-boundary rule cannot be enforced as written.

The canonical-block warning bullet points implementers to Q15 for details, but Q15 is about AdrAnchor/ConventionAnchor semantics and hash fields. The canonical-block unresolved decision is Q14, so this is a self-evidently wrong cross-reference.

D1.5 allows type catalog entries to reference conventions, but D3.2 says type catalogs have no convention reference field at all. D2.2 also lists `ConventionRef` use sites only for spec.json, so implementers cannot tell whether catalog `convention_refs` must exist and be verified or whether type-catalog-to-convention references are actually forbidden. This is a schema-level contradiction in the SoT reference policy.

The phase matrix still defines `Phase 3 完了` as `task-coverage.json` covering only all acceptance criteria and says merge is OK when SoT signals are blue. That conflicts with the stricter gate defined earlier: task coverage must cover both `in_scope` and `acceptance_criteria` with referential integrity, and final merge also requires every impl-plan task to be resolved. As written, this table can drive an implementation that permits a final merge with uncovered in-scope items or unresolved tasks.

This acceptance criterion is not satisfiable as written: it requires `informal_grounds[]` on type-catalogue entries to emit 🟡, but ADR D3.2 explicitly defers catalogue-side signal evaluation to a later ADR and only keeps the schema presence check in this scope. With only T003/T005 in scope, the track cannot meet this criterion unless you either narrow it to spec-side only or add a follow-on task for catalogue-side signal implementation.

The rendered graph still presents the approval/coverage model as live (`approved_at`, `effective_status`/`status`, `evaluate_coverage`, `task_refs`, `SpecStatus`, `CoverageResult`) even though the plan catalog deletes those concepts. That makes the plan-artifact set internally contradictory about the post-migration spec model; the graph and contract-map need to match the deleted-state intent or be explicitly marked legacy-only.

`TrackBlobReader` is declared with four methods in the usecase catalogue, but this contract map only renders two edges (`read_spec_document` and `read_track_metadata`). As written, the rendered map no longer describes the full port boundary and misstates the current contract surface.

The `ContentHash` entry describes the type as holding either a raw 32-byte buffer or a 64-character hex string. The ADR models `ContentHash` as a single normalized `[u8; 32]` value object, so this wording conflates constructor input formats with the stored domain representation.

`PlanArtifactRefsError` declares canonical-block suspicion as part of a public error enum, but the ADR/T011 design says canonical-block detection is warning-only. Implementing from this catalogue would turn an advisory warning into a fatal verifier error/CI failure, contradicting the planned gate semantics; canonical-block suspicion should be modeled as a warning finding instead of an error variant.

`SpecDocument` says every requirement, including `goal`, gains `id: SpecElementId` and the new ref fields, but the only modified requirement item contract (`SpecRequirement`) is documented as used only for `scope / constraints / acceptance_criteria`, and this catalogue declares no separate goal item type. That leaves the target representation for `goal[]` ambiguous and can let an implementation keep goals as bare strings while still following the `SpecRequirement` entry.

The `AdrRef` catalogue entry says ADR references are used in type catalogue entries, but the same plan’s SoT direction forbids type catalogue -> ADR direct references and `TypeCatalogueEntry` only adds `spec_refs` plus `informal_grounds`. Leaving this text in place gives implementers a contradictory schema target for catalogue entries.

The `ConventionRef` entry also claims use in type catalogue entries, while `TypeCatalogueEntry` explicitly says catalogue-level `convention_refs[]` is deferred to Q8 / a follow-up ADR. That contradicts the current-track scope, where catalogue entries should not gain convention reference fields yet.

ADR D1.4 defines identity-only metadata as retaining the track `status` field while moving only `tasks` and `plan` into `impl-plan.json`. This catalogue entry instead says TrackMetadata retains `status_override` and omits `status`; the matching TrackDocumentV2 entry repeats the same retained-field set. That gives implementers the wrong schema contract for metadata.json after task-derived status is removed, so the plan artifacts are internally inconsistent with the authoritative ADR.

`usecase::render_track_views` would move Markdown/view rendering and file I/O into the usecase layer, which is the wrong boundary for this repository's hexagonal split. The validation step can be shared, but the render pipeline should stay infrastructure-owned; otherwise this TODO would invert the dependency direction instead of just extracting the stale-signal check.

The new parenthetical still says the workflow's actual Markdown generation and file write happen "via infra port", which is inconsistent with the pure-orchestrator boundary described here and with the existing `contract_map_workflow.rs` pattern. As written, it can mislead implementers into reintroducing the same layer-boundary leak this TODO is trying to remove.
